key_points: Angles represent the amount of rotation between two rays meeting at a vertex
Angle relationships help determine properties of intersecting and parallel lines
The linear pair axiom states that adjacent angles forming a straight line sum to 180°
Vertically opposite angles are equal when two lines intersect
Parallel lines cut by a transversal create equal corresponding angles
Alternate interior angles are equal when lines are parallel
Interior angles on the same side of a transversal are supplementary
Angle relationships help in solving geometric proofs
Angle properties are widely used in triangles, polygons and geometric constructions
Understanding transversal angle relationships helps identify parallel lines
examples: Example of linear pair: angles 140° and 40°
Example of vertically opposite angles formed by intersecting lines
Example of corresponding angles when transversal cuts parallel lines
Example of alternate interior angles in parallel line diagrams
Example problem: if one angle is 70° in a linear pair, the other is 110°
Example problem: if vertically opposite angle is 65°, the opposite angle is also 65°
Example using angles around a point where sum equals 360°
Example of interior angles on the same side adding to 180°
Example identifying parallel lines using equal corresponding angles
formulas: Interior angles on same side → A + B = 180°
exam_tips: Use linear pair relationships to find unknown angles
Check vertically opposite angles first when solving intersection diagrams
Identify transversals carefully to determine angle relationships
If corresponding angles are equal, conclude the lines are parallel
Use the sum of angles around a point (360°) for multi-angle problems
Label diagrams clearly before solving angle equations
Break complex diagrams into simple angle pairs to solve easily
